impliedWarrantyOfHabitability.summary: North Carolina's Residential Rental Agreements Act requires landlords to comply with building and housing codes, keep the unit in a genuinely fit and habitable condition, maintain electrical/plumbing/heating systems, and keep smoke and carbon monoxide alarms working. A tenant's duty to pay rent and the landlord's duty to maintain the unit are legally linked, so a serious, uncured habitability breach can reduce what rent is actually owed.
impliedWarrantyOfHabitability.statuteName: North Carolina Residential Rental Agreements Act

covenantOfQuietEnjoyment.summary: North Carolina recognizes quiet enjoyment as a separate, judge-made protection implied into every lease, not a codified statute like habitability. It protects a tenant's peaceable possession against the landlord's own wrongful acts — not against noise or misbehavior by other tenants or outside parties — and it does not mean literal silence.

noticeToRemedy.summary: Tenants generally must give the landlord written notice of a needed repair before the landlord's repair obligation is triggered (except in emergencies), and the landlord then has a reasonable time to fix it based on the circumstances — North Carolina does not set one fixed general deadline. One specific exception: smoke and carbon monoxide alarms must be repaired or replaced within 15 days of written notice.

rentEscrowOrRepairDeduct.summary: North Carolina does not give tenants a self-help repair-and-deduct right, and a tenant may not unilaterally withhold rent. A tenant with an uncured habitability problem must go to court (or raise it as a defense in an eviction case) and ask a judge for remedies such as ordered repairs, rent abatement, or reimbursement of repair costs — the reduction only happens by court order, not by the tenant's own decision.

sources.label: N.C. Gen. Stat. § 42-42 — Landlord to provide fit premises
N.C. Gen. Stat. § 42-44 — General remedies, penalties, and limitations
N.C. Gen. Stat. Chapter 42, Article 5 — Residential Rental Agreements Act

internalNotes: TODO_VERIFY: covenant of quiet enjoyment in NC is common-law, not statutory — could not find a single codifying statute number, only case law referenced via a secondary source (Brennan Station 1671, LP v. Borovsky, 262 N.C. App. 1 (2018)); the opinion itself was not read directly.

TODO_VERIFY: the 'reasonable time' standard for general (non-alarm) repairs is the actual legal standard, not a research gap — it is genuinely open-ended/fact-specific rather than a fixed number of days.
